pattern: Picking exactly k pairs from two independently-increasing index sequences is a three-dimensional prefix DP: the state must remember how far along each array you are AND how many pairs you have already committed to, because a pair from early positions can still be worth more than one from late positions once sign flips are involved.
hints: This is not two-pointer greedy — because scores can be negative, the best pairing sometimes means skipping a locally-great product to preserve the ability to make later, even-better ones (or to avoid being forced into a bad one). Think about what state you need to remember as you scan both arrays.

Define dp[i][j][t] as the best score achievable using only the first i elements of nums1 and the first j elements of nums2, having committed to exactly t pairs so far. From dp[i][j][t] you can either not use nums1[i-1] (fall back to dp[i-1][j][t]), not use nums2[j-1] (dp[i][j-1][t]), or pair them up as the t-th pair (dp[i-1][j-1][t-1] + nums1[i-1]*nums2[j-1]).

Initialize dp[i][j][0] = 0 everywhere (using zero pairs always scores zero), and mark every dp[i][j][t] with t > 0 as unreachable whenever i == 0 or j == 0 (you cannot form a pair without elements on both sides) using a sentinel so far below any real achievable score that it can never win a max() by accident. The answer is dp[n][m][k].

edge_inputs.description: small mixed-sign case with a clear two-pair combination
different lengths, mixed signs, still k=2
k forces using every element of both (n=m=k=2); positive-times-negative products cannot be avoided
k=1 reduces the problem to picking the single best product pair anywhere in the grid
unequal array lengths (n=2, m=5) with all-positive values
k == n == m forces the identity pairing (only one increasing triple exists on each side); a negative-times-positive array pairing must yield a negative total
single element on each side at the value magnitude ceiling
n != m and k == min(n, m) forces every index of the shorter array to be used, while the longer array still has real choices to make
zero values mixed with positive and negative numbers, to catch a solution that mishandles multiplying by zero

stress.description: random n, m, k within bounds and random values across the full magnitude range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    cap = max(1, min(100, size))
    n = rng.randint(1, cap)
    m = rng.randint(1, cap)
    k = rng.randint(1, min(n, m))
    nums1 = [rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000) for _ in range(m)]
    return [nums1, nums2, k]

stress.description: maximum-size grid (n=m=k=100) to exercise the full O(n*m*k) DP for performance
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    cap = max(1, min(100, size))
    n = m = k = cap
    nums1 = [rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000) for _ in range(m)]
    return [nums1, nums2, k]

stress.description: all-negative nums1 against all-positive nums2 at moderate size, forcing many negative products while still leaving real index choices
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = m = max(2, min(60, size))
    k = rng.randint(1, min(n, m))
    nums1 = [-rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(m)]
    return [nums1, nums2, k]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def maxScore(self, nums1, nums2, k: int) -> int:
        n, m = len(nums1), len(nums2)
        NEG = -10 ** 18
        # prev holds dp[.][.][t-1]; start at t=0 where every state is reachable at score 0.
        prev = [[0] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
        for t in range(1, k + 1):
            cur = [[NEG] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
            for i in range(1, n + 1):
                row = cur[i]
                above = cur[i - 1]
                diag_row = prev[i - 1]
                a = nums1[i - 1]
                for j in range(1, m + 1):
                    best = row[j - 1]
                    if above[j] > best:
                        best = above[j]
                    diag = diag_row[j - 1]
                    candidate = diag + a * nums2[j - 1]
                    if candidate > best:
                        best = candidate
                    row[j] = best
            prev = cur
        return prev[n][m]
